participants.reasoning: Both descriptions report 28 participants with similar age and sex distribution, and the differences in group allocation and ethnic breakdown do not conflict with the extracted participant information.
participants.extracted: Twenty-eight patients with a clinical diagnosis of tuberous sclerosis complex were enrolled from the Tuberous Sclerosis Center of Excellence at the University of Texas Medical School at Houston between January and August 2010. All were older than 13 years (mean age 23 years); 15 were men and 13 were women. The sample was racially/ethnically diverse, including 72% non-Hispanic White, 7% Hispanic, 10% African American, 7% Asian, and 4% Native American participants. Exclusion criteria included pregnancy, current oral rapamycin use, and any immune dysfunction. Of the 28 enrolled, 23 completed the 6-month study; withdrawals were due to burning sensation with application, poor compliance, and one prolonged hospitalization unrelated to the study product.
participants.official: 28 participants (mean age 23 years old, ranging from 13 to > 30 years old, 15 males and 13 females) diagnosed with TSC based on the consensus criteria (Roach 1999). 18 participants in the treatment group and 10 participants in the placebo group. Five participants were withdrawn from the study, but it is unclear if they come from the treatment arm or from placebo arm.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted interventions match the official description in dose, topical route, three-arm randomization, 6-month treatment duration, and application/wash-off procedure, with only minor wording differences.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to apply one of three topical study products nightly for 6 months: placebo vehicle with no rapamycin, low-dose topical rapamycin 0.003% (1 mg per 30 cc) compounded with Skincerity®, or high-dose topical rapamycin 0.015% (5 mg per 30 cc) compounded with Skincerity®. The product was applied as a thin coating directly over the facial treatment area, allowed to air dry for 60 minutes, and washed off gently the following morning.
interventions.official:
Participants were randomised 3 arms.
topical (skin application) rapamycin 1 mg per 30 cc (0.003%)
topical (skin application) rapamycin 5 mg per 30 cc (0.015%)
topical (skin application) placebo
A thin coating of rapamycin was applied on the skin directly over the treatment area every morning (topical administration). The product was allowed to air dry for 60 minutes following application and was removed by gentle washing in the morning.
Participants were treated for 6 months.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official outcomes in measuring rapamycin concentrations and complete blood counts for safety and participant perception of skin appearance for efficacy, with adverse events also included officially.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: safety of topical rapamycin, assessed by serum rapamycin concentration (systemic absorption) measured monthly during the 6-month treatment period, with complete blood counts (white blood cell count, hemoglobin, platelet count) monitored monthly for evidence of systemic effects. Secondary outcome: efficacy of topical rapamycin for facial angiofibromas, assessed at study completion after 6 months by participant self-report of overall lesion appearance using the categories “got better on the treatment,” “got worse on the treatment,” or “the treatment made no difference.”
outcomes.official:
Effect of rapamycin on participants' perception towards their skin appearance following the treatment (the participants were asked if they "got better on the treatment", "got worse on the treatment" or if "the treatment made no difference")
Rapamycin concentrations and complete blood counts (including haemoglobin level and platelet count)
AEs
